coredata.dc:description: How to propel an efficient exploitation of waste streams is a pivotal tache for the long-range augment of hydrothermal biomass valorization. A facile approach was proposed to simultaneously produce carbon dots (CDs), fermentable sugar, and cellulose enzymatic lignin from agricultural straw with the aid of ionic liquid (IL, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium nitrate, [C2NH2MIm][NO3]) catalyzed hydrothermal treatment. The graphite N-doped CDs with bright-blue fluorescence, which was mainly derived from the incorporation of hemicellulose (e.g. xylooligosaccharides), lignin and [C2NH2MIm][NO3], exhibited an average-diameter of 8.14 nm. The exfoliation of amorphous parts and robust fibers was formed to improve cellulose digestibility from 14.7 to 81.6%. The efficient recovery and checkup of lignin pave a way for its potential depolymerization into arenes. This protocol offers a significant benefit for large-scale hydrothermal biorefinery where reduction of process waste is a prime concern, and leads to high-value products (i.e., CDs and lignin) that also fosters the feasibility of bioethanol.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 2D HSQC NMR spectra (a-c) of the depolymerized products in autohydrolysate. The yields of (d) XOS (DP 2–6) and (e) monosaccharide in autohydrolysate. The molecular distribution (f) of the liquid fraction from [C2NH2MIm][NO3]-catalyzed hydrothermal processing of wheat straw.
Schematic mechanism (a) for the preparation of CDs. The size distribution (b), PL stability (c), and proposed fluorescent mechanism (d) of CDs.

The Chemical components (a), enzymatic hydrolysis (b), and representative fundamental entities (c) of wheat straw. Control: original wheat straw. IHT: IL-catalyzed hydrothermal treatment of wheat straw.

The side-chain region (a) and aromatic region (b) of 2D HSQC NMR spectra, sugar analysis (c), and molecular weight (d) of cellulose enzymatic residual lignin (CERL). Ara: arabinose; Gal: galactose; Glu: glucose; Xyl: xylose; Man: mannose. Main classical structures of lignin: (A) β-O-4ʹ linkages; (Aʹ) Cα-oxidized β-O-4ʹ linkage substructures; (Aʹʹ) β-O-4ʹ linkages with p-coumaroylated γ-carbon; (B) resinol structures formed by β-βʹ/α-O-γʹ/γ-O-αʹ linkages; (C) phenylcoumarane structures formed by β-5ʹ/α-O-4ʹ linkages; (I) p-hydroxycinnamyl alcohol end groups; (G) guaiacyl unit; (S) syringyl unit; (Sʹ) oxidized syringyl unit linked a carbonyl or carboxyl group at Cα (phenolic); (H) p-hydroxyphenyl units; (pCA) free p-coumaric acid; (FA) ferulate.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: How to propel an efficient exploitation of waste streams is a pivotal tache for the long-range augment of hydrothermal biomass valorization. A facile approach was proposed to simultaneously produce carbon dots (CDs), fermentable sugar, and cellulose enzymatic lignin from agricultural straw with the aid of ionic liquid (IL, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium nitrate, [C2NH2MIm][NO3]) catalyzed hydrothermal treatment. The graphite N-doped CDs with bright-blue fluorescence, which was mainly derived from the incorporation of hemicellulose (e.g. xylooligosaccharides), lignin and [C2NH2MIm][NO3], exhibited an average-diameter of 8.14 nm. The exfoliation of amorphous parts and robust fibers was formed to improve cellulose digestibility from 14.7 to 81.6%. The efficient recovery and checkup of lignin pave a way for its potential depolymerization into arenes. This protocol offers a significant benefit for large-scale hydrothermal biorefinery where reduction of process waste is a prime concern, and leads to high-value products (i.e., CDs and lignin) that also fosters the feasibility of bioethanol.

ce:section.ce:para: Conversion of natural lignocellulose biomass into bio-based fuels, chemicals, and polymers is a highly desirable target for biorefinery, because it is a readily available, inexpensive and renewable resource (Ruiz et al., 2019). However, a core nodus of dominant bioethanol is how to tackle the inevitable resistance of lignocellulose for directly enzymatic saccharification. A break of “natural recalcitrance”, which involved with the tight bonding and close molecular packing of polymeric cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin in the plant cell walls, is the necessary prerequisite for achieving high enzymatic hydrolysis efficiency and the scale-up of a brand-new biorefinery era (Satlewal et al., 2018). In this vein, state-of-the-art pretreatments usually undergo efficient fractionation via the selective cleavage of hemicellulose and/or lignin for acquiring a high-level of cellulose-to-ethanol. Most pretreatments fall into one or a combination of the following categories: physical, chemical, and/or biological, which originally developed for ionic liquids (ILs) (Tu and Hallett, 2019), liquid hot water (Chen et al., 2018), and the like.

For large-scale process, a unique superiority of hydrothermal pretreatment is zero input of extra chemical under a relatively gentle environment. A hydrothermal process of sugarcane straw was optimized at 195 °C for 10 min to bring about an enhancement of cellulose bioconversion, in which the degradation of a remarkable hemicellulose (85.45%) and a low-level cellulose (9.80%) has taken place (Batista et al., 2019). Also, an isothermal hydrothermal pretreatment at 180 °C allowed a harvest of liquid fraction that was particularly rich of oligosaccharides and pentoses (Huang et al., 2019c). An alliance between hydrothermal (180 °C, 0.5 h) and alkaline ethanol extraction was conducted to sequentially pretreat wheat straw, leading to the generation of 61.69 g/kg total xylooligosaccharides (XOS) and 90.03% glucose (Chen et al., 2018). A novel hydrothermal pretreatment using high-pressure CO2 led to a reduction of peanut shells hemicellulose from 12.4% to 1.8%, which also facilitated an optimal enzymatic hydrolysis of 80.7% (Ge et al., 2019). The above-mentioned hydrothermal methods are principally intended to improve the amenability of enzymatic cellulose deconstruction, and induce the generation of XOS by altering biomass composition and cell wall structure. It should be emphasized that the economic profit of hydrothermal pretreatment was retarded by two key issues as below: 1) a strong demand on advanced separation and purification techniques is still the main bottleneck for the use of high-value small-size sugar derivate, especially for XOS; 2) the reckless discard of cellulose enzymatic residue as a process waste, which contains a great deal of useful lignin, is the common disposal during whole biorefinery. The produced XOS might be successfully recovered from autohydrolysate by purification technologies (e.g. membrane ultrafiltration, activated carbon adsorption, and flocculation), which still exhibit a remarkable potential in the fields of pharmaceuticals and food (Huang et al., 2019c). It is widely recognized that lignin holds the great promise for the alternative source of renewable aromatics, drop-in fuels and materials (Dong et al., 2019b). Therefore, the widespread utilization of waste autohydrolysate and recovery of cellulose enzymatic residual lignin (CERL) from hydrothermal lignocellulose becomes an urgent challenge to be faced.

Till now, the fusion of biomaterial and bioenergy opens up a new window for the all-round escalation of biorefinering industry (Xu et al., 2019a). Specifically, the facile transformation of autohydrolysate into value-added products offers a solution to the end-up issue of agricultural wastes. As new guiding light, a fruitful sugar-platform is the outgrowth of hydrothermal processing, which is most likely a useful and easily ignored resource for preparing carbon dots (CDs). To realize the development of “biowaste-to-wealth”, it is an alluring trend of directly translating carbohydrates and/or biomass into CDs for reducing waste resource (Liu et al., 2019). Typical CDs, an average particle dimension less than 10 nm, is the generic definition for a class of low-cost fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (Luo et al., 2019). Holding biocompatibility, high photoluminescence and stability, CDs are on their verge of deployment as one of the tactics for many aspects, e.g. biological, sensing, and optoelectronic fields (Xu et al., 2019b). For instance, the efficient fluorescence N-, S-, and Cl-doped CDs were fabricated by using glucose as a precursor for the sensitive recognition of [Fe(CN)6]4− (Hu et al., 2020). To unfold a high-value utilization of xylose, a novel xylose-mediated CDs with superior green-light was facilely synthesized to be a potential fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeit printing (Yang et al., 2019). The exploitation of biomass hydrothermal carbon into the fluorescent CDs was also fulfilled for the fluorescent Cu2+ sensor. (Zhao et al., 2019). Therefore, the sugar derivate from autohydrolysis not only provides the precursor of biochemicals, but also acts as the efficient carbon sources for the preparation of CDs (Huang et al., 2019a). The particle size and morphology of CDs derived from small-molecular carbohydrates can be easily manipulated by a hydrothermal process, whilst validly exploring the value of waste stream. As a typical class of green solvents, ILs have offered charming potentials to the sphere of homogeneous catalysis, biomass valorization, and CDs modification (Zhang et al., 2011; Zheng et al., 2019). Moreover, an easeful environment for the formation of fluorescent CDs might be created by an ally of xylan deacetylation and IL catalyst. Herein, we proposed an IL-aided hydrothermal treatment of lignocellulose for the synergistic outputs of CDs, enhanced enzymatic hydrolysis and residual lignin. The originality of this research can be classified into two crucial aspects: 1) prior to bioconversion of accessible substrate, the transformation of sugar derivate (e.g. XOS) into CDs was formed to meet the demand of facile autohydrolysate valorization; 2) an efficient recovery of CERL was coupled for endowing the delightful characteristics to biorefinery. It is believed that this progress spurs a focus on the use of waste streams (i.e., autohydrolysate and lignin CERL), eventually widening horizon for the development of CDs and biofuel, that will lead us closer to a quasi “waste-free” era of biorefinery.

Agricultural waste was harvested from Hubei province, China. The parent wheat straw was smashed to a particle size smaller than 0.5 mm, and then oven-dried at 75 °C for 48 h. The straw consisted of 40.9% cellulose, 26.8% hemicelluloses, and 20.5% lignin. All chemicals used were of analytical grade or best available. The IL, 1-aminoethyl-3-methylimidazolium nitrate ([C2NH2MIm][NO3]), was purchased from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Gansu Province, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionic liquid-catalyzed hydrothermal pretreatment, carbon dots preparation, and enzymatic hydrolysis
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] (1% v/v) as a catalyst, the hydrothermal pretreatment of wheat straw was conducted by a microwave irradiation device. The determination of XOS with a degree of polymerization (DP) of 2–6, monosaccharide, and molecular weights for autohydrolysate (Fig. 1 ) shed substantial lights on the manipulation of the fine structure of autohydrolysate-derived CDs. The content of XOS (DP 2–6) and monosaccharide was quantified by high performance anion exchange chromatography (HPAEC, Dionex ICS 3000) using a CarbopacTM PA-100 column (4 × 250 mm, Dionex). The weight-average (M w), number-average (M n) molecular weights and polydispersity (M w/M n) of autohydrolysate and lignin were determined by gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Agilent 1200) equipped with a differential refractive index detector (RID) (Xu et al., 2018). The N-doping of CDs was synthesized via the second-stage microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization of autohydrolysate, in which sugar derivate and [C2NH2MIm][NO3] mainly used as carbon precursor and heteroatom regulator respectively. The IL-catalyzed hydrothermally treated wheat straw, also called IHT, was used as a substrate in the process of enzymatic hydrolysis at the cellulase loading of 15 FPU/g (1:1 blend of Cellulase 1.5 L and Novozyme 188) to generate fermentable sugars and residual lignin (CERL). Lignin of 25 mg was dissolved in 0.5 mL DMSO‑d 6 to acquire its NMR spectrum on a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer (Xu et al., 2018).

It is underlined that the adoption of pressurized hot water is to partly tackle the robust resistance of lignocellulose, thus boosting the potential values for the efficient use of three building blocks. But the overall benefits from hydrothermal pretreatment and subsequent bioconversion of biomass are finite, especially for the agricultural straw with an intricate cell wall conformation (Ruiz et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2018). Moreover, the extended use of waste autohydrolysate from biomass is often limited by the lack of an effective method to realize the separation and/or purification of value-added intermediates (e.g. XOS, monosaccharide, degraded lignin, etc.). Herein, we creatively proposed a microwave hydrothermal process to pretreat virgin wheat straw in the presence of IL as the catalyst, which provides a favorable environment for the conversion of waste autohydrolysate into high-value nanomaterial. It also should be emphasized that the synergistic output of CDs, enhanced enzymatic digestion, and residual lignin (CERL) will be another bright spot. The coupled effect of [C2NH2MIm][NO3]-aided autohydrolysis during the whole dissociation of wheat straw was summarized into the following points: 1) [C2NH2MIm][NO3] can be potentially worked as the gentle acid catalyst to accelerate the xylan and/or lignin depolymerization, which is beneficial for varying and adjusting the carbon precursor of CDs; 2) importantly, [C2NH2MIm][NO3] not only provides the nitrogen doping atom and surface functional groups for the IL-mediated CDs, but also plays a critical role in tailoring the optical properties, size, and biocompatibility of CDs; 3) the advent of a readily digestible cellulose for enzymatic saccharification, and facile recovery of high-purity lignin. It is imaginable that the addition of acidic IL-[C2NH2MIm][NO3] would induce more attacks to lignocellulose cell wall, consequently leading to the accumulation of more tiny size CDs in combination with digestible cellulose and facilely returnable lignin.

During the hydrothermal process, a portion of carbohydrates can be degraded and then solubilized in water, which presents a starting resource for the synthesis of platform chemicals. Also, the chemical composition of precursors derived from lignocellulose is one of the vital factors that affect the preparation and properties of highly fluorescent heteroatom-doped CDs. First of all, a detailed analysis of the lyophilized autohydrolysate could initiate an in-depth comprehension of the chemical evolution and [C2NH2MIm][NO3] function for lignocellulose during the IL-catalytically hydrothermal process. A previous work allowed the selective extraction of a hemicellulose-rich pulp by using IL/water co-solvent system, inferring that the reduction of hydrogen-bond-accepting ability of IL anions would strengthen the selective dissolution of xylan to a certain extent (Froschauer et al., 2013). From this point of view, it is deemed that the IL-[C2NH2MIm][NO3] has the ability to catalyze the degradation of xylan over a hydrothermal system, thus accelerating the formation of oligomer from hemicellulose. As shown in Fig. 1a-c, the transitional fate and structure of the depolymerized products for autohydrolysate was identified by a 2D HSQC NMR method. The specific definition and tentative assignments of 13C-1H cross-signals in the HSQC spectra were also presented. From Fig. 1a of aliphatic region, the apparent signal of methoxyl group (–OCH3) was readily located at δC/δH 55.7/3.71. The signals of C–H in methoxyl (–OCH3) of 4-O-Me-α-d-GlcpA units (MeGlcA) and α-d-glucuronic acid (GlcA) appeared at δC/δH 59.10/3.54 and 67.50/3.55 (C5–H5) respectively, which obliquely suggested the peel off and/or oxidation of uronic acid. Notably, the response of xylan-derived signals was directly determined as below (Xu et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2017): 1) internal xylan of (1 → 4)-linked β-d-Xylp units at δC/δH 72.00/3.15 (C2–H2), 74.0/3.20 (C3–H3), 75.05/3.50 (C4–H4), 62.87/3.25 (C5ax–H5ax), and 62.87/3.87 (C5eq–H5eq), respectively; 2) the location of β-d-xylan with non-reducing end was assigned to δC/δH 72.10/3.10 (C2–H2), 76.50/3.08 (C3–H3), 70.10/3.20 (C4–H4), 65.70/3.04 (C5ax–H5ax), and 65.70/3.64 (C5eq–H5eq) respectively; 3) the weak correlations of 2-O-acetyl-β-d-Xylp (C2–H2), 3-O-acetyl-β-d-Xylp (C3–H3), and 2,3-dimer-O-acetyl-β-d-Xylp were detected at 73.10/4.42, 74.87/4.76, and 71.10/4.55 ppm respectively, offering a direct evidence for the partial decoration of acetyl groups on the depolymerized xylan (Dong et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2018). It may be safely affirmed that the principal unit of straw hemicelluloses was acetylated 4-O-methylgluconoxylan, in which the adjunction of acetyl groups was always presented on the C2 and C3 positions of backbone; 4) a puny signal at δC/δH 74.50/2.90 was mainly stemmed from C2–H2 of (1 → 4)-β-d-xylan with reducing end, implying the occurrence of oxidative degradation of xylan during the IL-catalyzed hydrothermal process. A substantial appearance of xylan-oriented signals potentially inferred that the main backbone of xylan was peculiarly prone to irreversible depolymerization. The origination of (1 → 4)-β-d-glucan was located at 72.90/2.88 (C2–H2), 70.80/3.15 (C4–H4, non-reducing end) and 61.10/3.55 ppm (C6–H6), which arose from the slight degradation of cellulose. As expected, the C–H signals at δC/δH 71.12/3.51 (C3–H3), 73.05/3.30 (C3–H3), and 73.0/4.90 (3-O-acetyl) were triggered by the response of α-d-mannan, α-d-galactan, and acetyl-β-d-mannan (Chen et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016).

For the region of polysaccharide anomerics (Fig. 1b), the correlations at δC/δH 92.2/4.71 (α-d-Glcp/xylp), 93.8/4.69 (α-d-manp), 97.1/5.11 (4-O-MeGlcA), 97.3–101.9/4.2 (β-d-xylp), 100.8/4.5 (β-d-manp), 101.7/4.86 (α-d-GalA), 103.2/4.1 (β-d-Glcp), and 105.3/4.26 (β-d-Galp) provided a direct proof on the existence of xylan, mannan, uronic acid, galactan and glucan, corresponding to the exfoliation of branch and collapse of backbone from hemicellulose (Wang et al., 2016). The start point for xylan branch to peel off could be affirmed by the appearance of saccharide signals, such as mannan and galactan. The extent of cellulose degradation might be alleviated by adjusting the IL-aided hydrothermal environment, while allowing the extraction of xylan for the generation of oligosaccharides. The conversion of hemicelluloses to small molecular intermediates can not only deliver an easily digestible cellulose, but also provide the substantial carbohydrate serving as the carbon precursor of CDs.

It has been declared that the irreversible hindrance of lignin aggradation to the productivity of enzyme was a critical problem during the process of cellulose hydrolysis. In the aromatic region of 2D HSQC spectra for autohydrolysate (Fig. 1c), the response of dissociated lignin was clearly recognized at δC/δH 104.0/6.60, 106.5/7.13, 111.2/6.92, 114.5/6.6–6.9 and 119.1/6.72, which corresponded to S2,6, oxidized S′2,6, G2, G5, and G6 units respectively (Wen et al., 2015). It is of great concern to point out that the sectional dissolution of degraded and/or oxidized lignin was taken place. On the other hand, the S and G motifs of lignin were easily apt to be interrupted as compared with H-type. The resonance at δC/δH 55.8/3.71, corresponding to the appearance of –OCH3, could also be responsible for the demethoxylation reaction of lignin. Albeit to a lower extent, the resonance of condensed lignin, affected by the IL-enlarged acidic hot water environment, was explicitly located. In short, the [C2NH2MIm][NO3] during the hydrothermal process not only acts as an acidic catalyst to propel the lignocellulose fractionation, but also offers the partial capacity of hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor for disrupting the inter-linkages between carbohydrate and/or lignin. In the initial period of hydrothermal process, lignin deconstruction was catalyzed by an ally of weak organic acid and [C2NH2MIm][NO3]. IL-aided acidic environment triggered an oxidation for attacking lignin linkages (i.e., β–O–4′ ether, C–C and hydrogen bonds), thereby yielding quasi nano-sized segments that was decorated with various oxidized functional groups (i.e., OH, COOH, C–O–C, C=O, NO3) on surface. Subsequently, the mechanism behind formation of CDs from lignin fragments involved the carbonization of its phenylpropane constituents by aldol condensation and/or cyclo addition. A rupture nucleation has taken place to form CDs, when the concentration of aromatic clumps reached a critical saturation.

The main sugar derivate of monomeric and oligomeric xylose was observed from the initially hydrothermal process liquid, owing to the xylan backbone of hemicellulose. Fig. 1d-e summarizes the yield of prebiotic XOS with a DP of 2–6, and monosaccharide derived from the IL-catalyzed hydrothermal processing of straw. The yield of XOS (DP of 2–6) was 49.73 mg/g with 15.58 mg/g xylobiose, 11.87 mg/g xylotriose, 8.69 mg/g xylotetraose, 7.42 mg/g xylopentaose, and 6.17 mg/g xylohexaose, accounting for 5.81%, 4.43%, 3.24%, 2.77%, and 2.30% of original xylan respectively. This also suggested the xylan was efficiently removed by hydrolyzing it into soluble oligo- and monosaccharides as a function of IL-catalyzed hydrothermal pretreatment. XOS are usually produced from xylan-rich lignocellulose by hydrothermally breaking down some ether bonds in the xylan backbone to give compounds with a lower DP. Autohydrolysis generally involves the deacetylation of xylan to produce acetic acid, which creates a weak acid environment to hydrolyze the hemicelluloses from lignocellulose. Also, the presence of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] could make the aid to construct an atmosphere of mild acid, and offer a limited hydrogen bonding to partially occupy the β-(1 → 4) glycosidic bonds, thereby accelerating the dissociation of xylan for the formation of oligomers (i.e., XOS). A comparable result of XOS (DP 2–6) yield (18.6 vs 17.3% of xylan) was obtained from the autohydrolysis of hazelnut shell, which suggested that the yield could be fine-tuned by the temperature and time (Surek and Buyukkileci, 2017). However, a XOS (DP 2–6) yield of 18.6% was lower than that of 25.87% from the hydrothermal fractionation of sweet sorghum stems (Sun et al., 2015), which was partially ascribed to the presence of acidic IL in this work. Under the IL-catalyzed environment, the XOS and monosaccharides might be excessively converted into the small molecular dehydration products, such as acetic acid, HMF, furfural, levulinic acid, and lactic acid (Lai et al., 2019). Hence, the coexistence of above sugar derivates could offer a breeding ground (e.g. affluent low M w carbon resource, mild acidic environment) for the effective preparation of CDs.

The [C2NH2MIm][NO3] held a dual role of homogeneous catalyst and co-solvent, resulting in the improved phase homogeneity and depolymerization efficiency. The use of IL as catalysts in the hydrothermal processing of biomass could alter the selectivity of fractionation, which preferred to yield low molecular derivate (e.g. monosaccharides) via the hydrolysis of biopolymers. Xylose was the dominant monosaccharide (44.65 mg/g), mainly due to a chain of peeling off, degradation, and dissolution of hemicelluloses. As opposed to XOS, the weak acid environment enabled the major degradation of xylan into xylose. The existence of second major glucose reached up to about 15.32 mg/g, suggesting the IL-catalyzed hydrolysis of hemicellulosic branch and/or cellulose. Moreover, the soluble yield of galactose was 14.81 mg/g, signifying that the galactan side-chain was lightly peeled off from xylan backbone during this IL-assisted process. Small amounts of rhamnose (4.52 mg/g), arabinose (4.45 mg/g), mannose (5.52 mg/g), and glucuronic acid (1.13 mg/g), which are other minor hemicellulosic components, were also determined. It is expected that these sugars would be initially dehydrated into inhibitors (e.g. HMF, furfural, etc.) and then reacted with each other under the catalysis of IL to produce soluble aromatic clusters, ultimately leading to the growth of carbogenic nuclei of CDs. An isothermal autohydrolysis of rapeseed straw was conducted by a previous report, which summarized that the enrichment of small molecular derivates (e.g. low-DP XOS, etc.) in the liquid fraction was the direct fuse for an average molecular weight ranging from 2130 to 8430 g/mol (Wang et al., 2016). Herein the average molecular weight of autohydrolysate was 6800 g/mol (Fig. 1f), which occupied a crucial role in contributing to the subsequent preparation of CDs, i.e., morphology, size, optical properties. In particular, the M w is a direct expression of the molecular size of degraded items that strongly tailored the particle size distribution and surface states of CDs, thereby endowing a homologous photoluminescence of CDs with specific emission. The response of a relatively high polydispersity index (M w/M n, 3.7) for autohydrolysate could be attributed to the heterogeneous phase composition containing a wide range of degraded products. Note that, the physicochemical features of CDs were highly relied on the concomitance of carbohydrates, lignin fractions, and [C2NH2MIm][NO3] in autohydrolysate.

It should be emphasized that the successful separation and purification is an essential prerequisite for deriving the value-added intermediates (e.g. XOS, HMF) from autohydrolysate. Herein, the facile preparation of CDs from waste autohydrolysate paved a new avenue for the feasibility of hydrothermal-driven biorefinery. A recent report has figured out that the hydrophilicity and fluorescence nature of CDs are highly bound up with alkyl side chain length of imidazolium cationic moiety (Guo et al., 2019a). The nitrogen-containing alkyl chain of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] catalyst might act as a medium to passive the CDs and modify the surface defects of N-doped CDs. Fig. 2 a illustrates the scheme of the CDs fabrication. During IL-assisted hydrothermal process, the weak acidolysis released the degraded motifs such as XOS, monosaccharide and organic acid, as well as synchronously dissolved the lignin fragment. Through the regulation of [C2NH2MIm][NO3], the abovementioned molecules were conducted to synthesize N-doped CDs involving dehydration, polymerization/condensation, aromatization and carbonization (Fig. 2a). It should also be emphasized that IL-[C2NH2MIm][NO3] must take part in the formation of the N-doped CDs. Briefly, the main lignocellulose component of hemicellulose was firstly degraded and/or dehydrated into small molecular moieties (e.g. monosaccharide, XOS, HMF, etc.) under the acidic environment, and then broken down into individual carbogenic nanoparticles. This process could be accelerated by the presence of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] catalyst in the aqueous medium with the purpose of dehydrating organic matter and heteroatom doping. Afterwards, the auxiliary passivation of surface defect sites in carbogenic nanoparticles was achieved using amine-terminated IL to yield luminescent CDs (Peng and Travas-Sejdic, 2009). To confirm the nature of nanoparticles, morphological characterization revealed that the as-prepared CDs were stably monodispersed nanodots without any significant aggregation. A small quantity of lignin droplets, with innate fluorescence property, existed in the mixture of sugar deviates also has an influence on the particle size and properties of CDs. An average diameter of 8.14 nm (Fig. 2b), an important size indicator of sp2 domains, was calculated for CDs, which is always associated with the energy-gap changes (Luo et al., 2019). To illuminate the CDs stability, the impact of UV duration on PL intensity is displayed in Fig. 2c. A slight decrease of the PL intensity of CDs was observed during continuous UV exposure of about 80 min, which implied that the surface states of CDs are erratic under abrupt UV irradiation. To a certain extent, CDs held a decent resistance to photo-bleaching, owing to the stable PL intensity under the irradiation time from 100 to 200 min. In most cases (Fig. 2d), the O-containing groups on sp2-hybridized carbon can induce significant local distortion and then create various energy levels in the π-π* gap. These energy gaps induced by O-related defect state are more likely to be predominant due to the existence of C=O bonds. The electrons from the ground state are excited to the different vibrational energy levels of π* by different excitation. Subsequently, some excited electrons trapped by the corresponding O-related defect state, and finally transition back to the ground state through different radiant recombination paths, thus giving rise to the excitation dependent blue emission (Yang et al., 2019). In addition, the surface-contained amino groups play a leading role in the redshift of PL emission. The narrowed energy gap created by the protonated amino groups (termed N-related defect state) in CDs is the intrinsic cause of green PL emission (Yang et al., 2019). Thus, the final bright-blue emission of CDs suggested that the O-related defect state (blue emission) outplayed N-related defect state (green PL emission).

From X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, the interlayer spacing (0 0 2) of graphitic structure was confirmed by the presence of a wide peak at around 22.5° and a minor peak at 43.0°. An enlarged interlayer distance, corresponding to the broad peak at 22.5° of d spacing of 0.41 nm, was triggered by the plan distortion from sp3 C and/or steric hindrance of functional groups on the edge. On the other side, the characteristic (0 0 2) diffraction of graphite responded at 43.0°, deduced that the interlayer stacking of CDs was roughly analogous to that of graphite (~0.35 nm). Hence, an intense packing of highly conjugate sp2 plot occupied a major part in the kernel of CDs. For the frontier of CDs, the interlayer distance would be enlarged by the functional groups and/or sp3 C (Miao et al., 2018). The strong effect of quantum confinement, zigzag sites, and surface state on the emission properties of CDs has been pointed out (Tian and Yin, 2019). Herein, we try to tentatively correlate their emission properties with the different scale of nitrogen and oxygen in the arrays of C=N/C=O. A bright blue fluorescence of the as-prepared CDs dispersion was observed under UV irradiation. The location of the absorption bands of CDs was usually assigned to the π–π* transition of the well-defined conjugate aromatic structures (Zhao et al., 2019), as well as, the n–π* transition of conjugated C=O and C=N. The maximum emission of CDs was located at about 450 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, inferring that the emission was mainly launched from the n–π* transition of aromatic sp2 system comprising C=O and C=N bonds (Baker and Baker, 2010). The size distribution, functionalized surface, salvation effect, and/or emissive traps of CDs are usually classified as the origin of their excitation-dependent behavior (Zhao et al., 2019). Due to the uniform size of CDs, the defects and/or vacancies from oxygen/nitrogen functional groups may initiate the unique excitation-dependent behavior of CDs. A representative fluorescence property of CDs is heavily dependent on the wavelength of excitation, illuminating their prospect in the territory of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. A gradual red-shifts from 420 to 500 nm for the emission peak was observed, while increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 400 nm. A strong PL emission peak appeared at 450 nm under 360 nm excitation, fully matching with the PL excitation/emission spectrum, which is sharply evocative of diverse emissive trap sites of CDs (Hu et al., 2017). The C=O and C=N functional groups on the surface of CDs was conjectured as a central reference for their blue-color emission. Thus, the introduction of new energy-levels into their electronic structures as well as the generation of affluent structure conformations lead to a further possibility of electronic transition (Nie et al., 2014). Raman spectrum was plotted to clearly investigate the defect state and crystalline quality, which provides a reference for clarifying the former deduction. The CDs exhibited the well-defined D-band and G-band at about 1340 and 1570 cm−1, respectively (Hu and Gao, 2020). A graphitic G band was indicative of the sp2 carbon atoms, however, the D band was originated from the structural distortion of graphitic sp2 carbon layers with defects, such as sp3 coordination or small varieties in particle size of CDs. The I D /I G ratio of CDs was about 1.013, implying the presence of disorder or defects in the graphitized structure with the sp2 carbon networks. Additionally, a slice of weak peaks at about 1100, 1300, and 1500 cm−1 are also observed, which could be attributed to pyrrole-ring breathing, C–C/C–O–C, and N–N signals, respectively (Sun et al., 2016).

To revive the role of functional groups, two main techniques of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform-infrared spectra (FTIR) were applied to explore the structure of CDs. XPS is usually applied to cross-check the valance state and surface composition of CDs. We introduce the wide range survey XPS spectrum of CDs, which are clearly consisted of C, O, and N elements. We also illuminate the high-resolution C1s, O1s and N1s XPS spectra of CDs, respectively. The configuration of carbon was governed by the sp2 hybridized C–C bonds with minor existence of carboxyl (–COOH) and hydrocarbon groups (Wang et al., 2019). The high-resolution C1s spectrum can be divided into four kinds of C species, including –COOH at 289.6 eV, C=O at 286.3 eV, sp3 C (C–O and C–N) at 285.5 eV, and sp2 C (C=C/C–C) at 284.8 eV. The O1s pattern is fitted into two constituents located at 531.9 and 533.1 eV, which are associated with C=O and C–OH/C–O–C bonds, respectively (Miao et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2016). For N1s, it can be deconvolved into pyridinic N (399.8 eV), amino/graphitic N (401.0 eV), and pyrrolic (401.7 eV) respectively (Jiang et al., 2015; Sheng et al., 2011), strongly proving the successful doping of nitrogen due to the participation of N-rich [C2NH2MIm][NO3] in hydrothermal procedure. The N-involved functional groups adhering on the CDs surface was fulfilled to offer a guarantee of its luminescent. The functional groups presented on the surface of the CDs was identified by FTIR, which provides a clue about the relationship between structure and stability. The FTIR profile of N-doped CDs was collected in the range of 800 and 3800 cm−1 to differentiate the specific absorption bands. It is directly indicated that the advent of conjugated aromatic structure was accompanied by the O- and N-related groups. The location of a broad peak at about 3300 cm−1 was stemmed from the stretching vibration of OH and N–H, giving a sign of hydroxyl (−OH) and amino (−NH2) functional groups adhering to the surface of CDs (Sun et al., 2016). The analyses of XPS spectra also can support the FT-IR results. The bands at 2930 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 were triggered by the stretching of methylene C–H and –COOH, respectively (Yang et al., 2018). The two characteristic peaks centered at 1410 and 1010 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of C–O–C and C=C, respectively. The peaks between 1350 and 1250 cm−1 could be ascribed to N–H and C–N groups (Bouzas-Ramos et al., 2019). Moreover, the peak at around 1100 cm−1 could be corresponded to stretching modes of C–O–C and C–O functional groups. In brief, the FTIR results indicated the concomitance of polymorphic functional groups on the surface of CDs, in which the oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups afford an excellent dispersion and stabilization of CDs in water (Bouzas-Ramos et al., 2019). Clearly, the as-prepared CDs were tentatively considered to be composed of nano-scaled graphite-like heterocycle skeleton/core with a rich of –COOH, –OH, and –NH2 functional groups on the surface, which enabled their specific fluorescence. The quantum yields of IL-modified CDs are closely related with both the cationic and anionic moieties of ILs, i.e., longer side chains of cations in the imidazolium ILs and weakly nucleophilic anions tend to produce highly fluorescent CDs (Zheng et al., 2019). The PL quantum yield of CDs reached up to as high as 11.7%, which is highly referred to their conjugated π-domains of carbon core and functional groups on the surface. The fluorescence performance of CDs was attributed to the coexistence of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] and low molecular derivates under the hydrothermal condition, which plays a main duty in the resultant of particle size, surface state and hydrophilicity. This method not only bridges the gap between underutilized hydrothermal wastes with fluorescence CDs, but also effectively avoids a poison to the late performance of enzymatic saccharification and lignin recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural properties and enzymatic hydrolysis of straw
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal pretreatment of lignocellulose with a certain IL as catalyst was highly efficient for delivering a substrate that can be easily saccharified into fermentable glucose with a high yield. In specific, three building blocks of cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose were intensely cross-coupled to sustain a compact plant architecture by tight linkages, which usually retarded the enzymatic digestion (Guo et al., 2019b). Thus, the bioconversion yield of cellulose-to-glucose highly relied on the variation of components. From Fig. 3 a, the absolute content of original straw was 40.9% for cellulose, 26.8% for xylan, and 20.5% for lignin. After IL-aided hydrothermal pretreatment, the appreciable increase of cellulose content (40.9% vs 66.1%) was mainly due to the substantial peeling of hemicellulose (90.5% removal), leading to an incrassation of cellulose and/or lignin. As expected, a slight loss of cellulose (4.5%) was mostly governed by the direct hydrolysis of amorphous cellulose. A delignification of 16.9% for the hydrothermally treated sweet sorghum bagasse (180 °C, 30 min) was observed by a recent report (Khalili and Amiri, 2019). In this study, a higher degree of delignification was 28.5%, which potentially improved by the addition of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] catalyst during autohydrolysis stage. The partial removal of lignin was actualized by the homolytic depolymerization of β-aryl ether bonds, thereby releasing the low-molecular chain of soluble aromatics.

The morphology of fiber (i.e., available surface area and porosity) was one of the major driving forces affecting the efficiency of enzymatic hydrolysis. The original straw fiber exhibited an intact and compact morphology that was adverse for enzyme permeation. The corrosive and loose structure of IHT offered a forcing house for allowing enzymes to be easily embedded and hydrolyzed cellulose substrate. As it is widely known, an obvious reduction in crystallinity index (CrI) associated with a transition of cellulose I to II is the typical characteristic of IL-involved lignocellulose processing (Brandt et al., 2013). Herein, the IL-catalyzed hydrothermal pretreatment of straw led to semi-amorphous structure and retained primarily a cellulose I lattice with a higher CrI degree (45.1 vs 32.7%). After hydrothermal pretreatment with a lower percentage of [C2NH2MIm][NO3] catalyst, the CrI evolution (45.1 vs 32.7%) suggested that the elimination of amorphous constituents (a rise of CrI) outplayed the dissolution/swelling of cellulose (a decrease of CrI). Therefore, the increase of CrI was mainly triggered by the substantial removal of amorphous hemicellulose, which positively influenced the leakage of cellulose substrate available for cellulase attack.

The inherent recalcitrant and low accessibility of virgin straw was proved by a weak conversion of 14.7% cellulose into glucose (Fig. 3b). As expected, a greater conversion yield (81.6%) of straw was observed for IHT, which was approximately 5.6-folds enhancement for raw material. The mechanism behind the efficient enzymatic hydrolysis of straw during the [C2NH2MIm][NO3]-catalyzed hydrothermal process may be determined by two driving forces: 1) removal of the amorphous portion involving with the elimination of main hemicellulose, which led to an increase of CrI; 2) the clear disruption of robust plant cell wall to expose cellulose amenable to enzymes (Huang et al., 2019b).

For conventional bioethanol industry, lignin is usually discarded as a waste stream after cellulose enzymatic hydrolysis. However, the conversion of lignin into small-molecular arenes as commodity chemicals and/or drop-in fuels is a highly desirable objective for the renaissance of biorefinering (Dong et al., 2019b). Two dimensional HSQC NMR spectra, associated sugars, and molecular weight distribution of CERL are deciphered in Fig. 4 . The main substructures of lignin and their related assignments of 13C–1H cross signals were also presented. For the side-chain region (Fig. 4a), the identification of chief linkages in lignin containing methoxy group (–OCH3), β–O–4ʹ (A), β–βʹ/α–O–γʹ/γ–O–αʹ linked resinol structures (B), and β–5ʹ/α–O–4ʹ assembled phenylcoumaran motifs (C) were facilely located (Wen et al., 2015). The peak of –OCH3 in this region might be potentially stemmed from the CH3–O–R of methoxylated alkanes/esters and the saturation of phenolics (McClelland et al., 2019). In addition, the Cγ–Hγ in the p-hydroxycinnamyl alcohol end groups (I) was explicitly detected (Wen et al., 2014). The signals from β-d-xylopyranoside (xylp) were clearly noted at δC/δH 102.3/4.31 (C1–H1), 72.8/3.06 (C2–H2), 73.8/3.28 (C3–H3) and 76.0/3.58 (C4–H4) respectively (Wen et al., 2013), which was consistent with the compositional analysis of about 5% residual xylan sticking to lignin. From the aromatic region (Fig. 4b), the recognition of guaiacyl (G), syringyl (S), oxidized syringyl unit with a carbonyl group at Cα (Sʹ), p-hydroxyphenyl (H) units, p-coumaric acid (pCA) and ferulic acid (FA) was clearly affirmed to draw a close analogy with general grassy lignin (Dong et al., 2019a; Wen et al., 2013). As shown, the inter-unit β–O–4ʹ bonding still occupied a lot of space for CERL that was of a specific G/H/S character. For the purity of lignin (Fig. 4c), the total content of associated carbohydrate was 11.4 wt% based on dry lignin, which was highly referred to the leftover cellulose and hemicellulose after the whole process. It can also be regarded that the primary monosaccharide was xylose (4.45%) and glucose (3.52%), while a small quantity of arabinose (1.21%), galactose (1.18%), and mannose (1.04%) was remained in CERL. A higher content of xylose could be attributed to the presence of xylan in the potential lignin-carbohydrate complex (LCC) (Dong et al., 2020). The existence of glucose was involved with a small amount of stubborn cellulose that was incompletely degraded during the process of enzymatic hydrolysis (Wen et al., 2015). The M w value (3100 g/mol) and polydispersity index (M w/M n, 1.23) of CERL, calculating from the GPC profile, are appeared in Fig. 4d. To a certain extent, the M w of CERL was related to the partial collapse of aryl ether bonds between structural entities of lignin during IL-catalyzed autohydrolysis.

The IL-[C2NH2MIm][NO3] presented a dual role of homogeneous catalyst and heteroatom-doping, which accounts for the efficient deconstruction of straw and surface functionalization of CDs. Participation of sugar derivate, lignin fragment and [C2NH2MIm][NO3] was coupled in the creation of graphite N-doped CDs with bright-blue fluorescence, which exhibited a well-defined spherical shape with an average diameter of 8.14 nm. The collapse of natural recalcitrance facilitated cellulose digestibility (81.6%) and lignin recovery. This facile approach delivers the enlightening insights to generate value-added items (i.e., CDs and lignin) from biomass, and also sheds light on the rational design of complete lignocellulose valorization for “waste-to-wealth”.
